title: Cat Drooling Excessively: Causes, Triage, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat drooling? Learn what causes excessive salivation in cats, when it is an emergency, and how photos help with veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive drooling in cats often indicates an underlying medical issue like dental disease or toxicity and usually requires a vet visit within 24 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if drooling is paired with breathing difficulties or suspected toxin ingestion.

What causes excessive drooling in cats and how is it defined?

Excessive drooling, clinically known as ptyalism, occurs when a cat produces more saliva than they can swallow or when they are physically unable to swallow properly. While some cats drool occasionally when they are extremely relaxed or happy, sudden and heavy drooling is typically a sign of an underlying medical issue, such as:

  • Dental disease
  • Oral injuries
  • Ingestion of a toxin
  • Nausea

When is my cat's drooling considered a medical emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. In most cases, excessive drooling is not an immediate life-threatening emergency, but it does require a veterinary appointment within 24 hours. However, you should seek emergency veterinary care immediately if the drooling is accompanied by:

  • Difficulty breathing or gasping
  • Suspected ingestion of a toxic substance (such as a poisonous plant or chemical)

How does a photo or video help a professional triage my drooling cat?

If you are consulting with a triage professional, providing a clear photo or short video of the drooling can be incredibly helpful. Visuals allow a professional to perform the following actions:

  • Assess the color and consistency of the saliva
  • Check for swelling or visible foreign objects in the mouth
  • Observe your cat's overall posture and distress level
  • Determine the severity of the situation and required speed of care

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive drooling in cats can be caused by a variety of factors, including oral foreign bodies, neoplasms, injuries, mucosal defects, infectious diseases such as rabies, neurologic disorders, motion sickness, fear, reluctance to swallow due to esophageal irritation or obstruction, sublingual lesions, tonsillitis, administration of medication, conformational defects, metabolic disorders like hepatic encephalopathy or uremia, and salivary gland issues. Halitosis and blood-tinged saliva are common signs associated with caudal stomatitis or glossitis. Anorexia may also be present, particularly in cats. The possibility of rabies should be eliminated before performing an oral examination.

Chapter: Neurology, Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease, Dentistry, Emergency, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 368)

faqs.question: Can dental problems cause my cat to drool?
faqs.answer: Yes, dental issues like gingivitis, tooth resorption, or an abscess are among the most common causes of excessive salivation in cats.

faqs.question: Does stress cause cats to drool?
faqs.answer: Some cats may drool excessively during high-stress situations, such as car rides or visits to the groomer, due to anxiety or motion sickness.

faqs.question: What should I look for in my cat's mouth?
faqs.answer: If it is safe to do so, check for redness, bleeding, broken teeth, or any foreign objects stuck in the gums or roof of the mouth.
